If your microphone is 8, 10, 24 inches away from you, it's picking up a lot of room noise that you'll do better if you get it right up close to your mouth. Pop filters. A pop filter is to prevent what we call a plosive, which is what happens when you say a P or a T. So anytime that you say those letters, there's an explosive amount of air that comes out of your mouth at that particular point in time. That's called a plosive. In microphones, that tends to overload the microphone for a split second, and you can definitely hear it. One of the things we do is we put on pop filters on top of our microphone to prevent that explosion of air from actually hitting the screen on the microphone where it records. I actually use two. It just happens anytime you're presenting in front of a microphone. All right, so that's well lit, well-miked, and then I like to be well insulated. So I love kids. These are not my kids, by the way. ## [12:09 - 15:11]

So some people do standing desks. Personally, I not trained on standing desks. And so I don't do well standing for two hours. And so what I do is I sit on the edge of my seat when I'm presenting. Another aspect of physical preparation is to block up and block off enough time to do your presentation completely. So make sure you don't have a deadline forcing you to end at a certain time. You're going to want to use the time that you need to to be able to do your cell with no hard deadline on the other end. I literally cut cells on a webinar once because I had to cut it off because I had another appointment that had to happen. And it just was a stupid idea. Make sure you got yourself a powerful computer. As I have talked so I've talked with the CEOs of many of the companies that run the different webinar software and I asked them why does your software keep failing? And the answer that I get back over and over again is that most of the problems with webinar software when webinar software fails, it's actually caused by the computer that you're using getting overloaded. If you think about it, a webinar is recording audio, video, dealing with bandwidth, both outgoing and incoming, and all of that and your presentation which is being made. Every one of those are heavy draws on the processor in your computer. And so if you have a low power processor in your computer or a computer that's been being running for a long time and so many of those are taken up because hey operating system don't always do a good job of dumping out all programs when you get done with them and freeing up that memory and freeing up that that part of the processor to be able to operate. And so the more powerful the computer you've got that does this, the better off your webinars tend to go. Personally, I have chosen that I almost never run webinars from a laptop. Why? ## [15:12 - 18:15]

When I try to present from a laptop, not quite as good. And so I just like to run webinars from desktops. I have more confidence. If you're confident in your laptop by all means do it.
